questions.question: Which Marsh-Oberhuber stage shows intraepithelial lymphocytic infiltration without villous atrophy?
questions.explanation: Marsh 1 is characterized by increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (>40 per 100 enterocytes) with preserved villous architecture, without crypt hyperplasia or atrophy.

questions.question: What is the distinctive histological finding of Marsh 3b in the Oberhuber classification?
questions.explanation: Marsh 3b (subtotal atrophy) presents shortened villi with a hair/crypt ratio <1:1, but still visible, differing from Marsh 3c (total) where they disappear completely.

questions.question: Which Marsh-Oberhuber stage combines crypt hyperplasia with partial villous atrophy?
questions.explanation: Marsh 3a (partial atrophy) is defined by shortened but still present villi, accompanied by crypt hyperplasia and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes.

questions.question: What architectural change defines Marsh 2 in the histological classification of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Marsh 2 is characterized by crypt hyperplasia (elongation and increased mitosis) without villous atrophy, along with intraepithelial lymphocytosis.

questions.question: What is the usual clinical correlation of Marsh 3c in duodenal biopsy?
questions.explanation: Marsh 3c (total atrophy) is the most advanced stage of mucosal damage in active celiac disease, associated with severe malabsorption and positivity of specific antibodies.

questions.question: What microscopic characteristic do Marsh 1 and Marsh 2 share in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Both stages present an increase in intraepithelial lymphocytes (>40/100 enterocytes); The difference is that Marsh 2 adds crypt hyperplasia, while Marsh 1 retains normal architecture.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial screening test for celiac disease in patients with clinical suspicion?
questions.explanation: Anti-transglutaminase IgA is the initial screening test due to its high sensitivity and specificity in patients with suspected celiac disease, as long as there is no IgA deficiency.

questions.question: What clinical condition can cause a false negative anti-transglutaminase IgA test?
questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common cause of false negative results in anti-transglutaminase IgA, so it is recommended to measure total IgA simultaneously to correctly interpret the result.

questions.question: What antibody should be requested as an alternative if the anti-IgA transglutaminase test is negative but high clinical suspicion persists?
questions.explanation: Deaminated anti-gliadin IgG is the recommended alternative when there is IgA deficiency or discordant results, since it maintains good sensitivity in these clinical scenarios.

questions.question: What is the approximate sensitivity of anti-transglutaminase IgA in untreated celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Anti-transglutaminase IgA has a sensitivity greater than 95% in untreated celiac disease, making it the first-line serological test for screening.

questions.question: What factor can reduce the specificity of anti-IgA transglutaminase in the diagnosis of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Autoimmune liver cirrhosis can give false positive results for anti-transglutaminase IgA, reducing its specificity, so clinical and histological correlation is required to confirm the diagnosis.

questions.question: What study is required to confirm celiac disease after a positive anti-IgA transglutaminase result?
questions.explanation: Duodenal biopsy obtained by endoscopy is the gold standard for confirming celiac disease, since it allows the evaluation of the characteristic villous atrophy and intraepithelial inflammation.

questions.question: Which HLA allele is most strongly associated with celiac disease?
questions.explanation: HLA-DQ2 is the most common and highest risk allele in celiac disease, present in approximately 90-95% of patients.

questions.question: What is the approximate percentage of celiac patients who express HLA-DQ2?
questions.explanation: About 90-95% of patients with celiac disease carry the HLA-DQ2 allele, making it the main genetic factor.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism relates HLA-DQ2 to celiac disease?
options: Presentation of gliadin to T lymphocytes
questions.explanation: HLA-DQ2 presents gliadin peptides deaminated to CD4+ T lymphocytes, triggering the immune response characteristic of celiac disease.

questions.question: What enzyme deaminates gliadin to facilitate its binding to HLA-DQ2?
questions.explanation: Tissue transglutaminase deaminates glutamine residues in gliadin, generating peptides with high affinity for HLA-DQ2.

questions.question: What is the main clinical utility of HLA-DQ2/DQ8 typing?
options: Rule out celiac disease if negative
questions.explanation: The absence of HLA-DQ2 and DQ8 has a high negative predictive value, allowing celiac disease to be ruled out with great certainty.

questions.question: What other HLA allele, besides DQ2, is associated with celiac disease?
questions.explanation: HLA-DQ8 is the second most common allele in celiac disease, present in the majority of patients who do not carry HLA-DQ2.

questions.question: What immunological deposit characterizes dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: Dermatitis herpetiformis is characterized by granular deposits of IgA in the dermal papillae, a pathognomonic finding that differentiates it from other bullous dermatoses.

questions.question: What is the first-line pharmacological treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: Dapsone is the treatment of choice to control the skin lesions of dermatitis herpetiformis, acting as an anti-inflammatory and reducing intense itching.

questions.question: What systemic disease is invariably associated with dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: Dermatitis herpetiformis is the cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease, present in approximately 25% of celiac patients, and shares its autoimmune basis.

questions.question: What is the cardinal symptom of lesions in dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: Intense itching, often accompanied by burning, is the main symptom of dermatitis herpetiformis, preceding the appearance of grouped vesicles.

questions.question: What diet constitutes the definitive treatment for dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: The gluten-free diet is the mainstay of long-term treatment, as it reverses both celiac enteropathy and the skin lesions of dermatitis herpetiformis.

questions.question: What anatomical location is most common in dermatitis herpetiformis?
questions.explanation: Dermatitis herpetiformis lesions typically appear on extensor surfaces such as elbows, knees, buttocks, and sacral region, with symmetrical distribution.

questions.question: What is the most frequent cause of false negatives in the serological diagnosis of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is common in patients with celiac disease and causes false negative results in tests that detect IgA anti-transglutaminase antibodies, since these patients do not produce enough IgA.

questions.question: What antibody should be requested if celiac disease with IgA deficiency is suspected?
questions.explanation: In patients with IgA deficiency, the test of choice is anti-transglutaminase IgG, as it avoids the false negative that occurs with IgA-based tests.

questions.question: What condition predisposes to false negatives in IgA serology for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the condition most associated with false negatives in IgA serology for celiac disease, since these patients do not generate the IgA antibody response necessary for the test.

questions.question: What is the approximate prevalence of IgA deficiency in patients with celiac disease?
questions.explanation: IgA deficiency occurs in approximately 5-10% of patients with celiac disease, a much higher prevalence than in the general population, justifying clinical suspicion of serological false negatives.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding suggests IgA deficiency in the setting of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is defined by serum levels of total IgA less than 7 mg/dL, which should be suspected in the event of negative serological results with high clinical suspicion of celiac disease.

questions.question: What complementary test is recommended in the event of negative serology but high suspicion of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Given negative serology and high clinical suspicion, it is recommended to quantify total serum IgA to rule out IgA deficiency, which would explain false negatives in anti-transglutaminase IgA antibody tests.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: Gluten ataxia is associated with anti-gliadin antibodies that cross-react with Purkinje cells of the cerebellum, provoking a humoral immune response.

questions.question: Which antibody is typically associated with gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: Anti-gliadin antibodies (especially IgG) are elevated in patients with gluten ataxia, distinguishing it from other neurological autoimmune causes.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: The strict gluten-free diet is the basis of treatment, since it eliminates the antigenic trigger and can stabilize or improve neurological symptoms.

questions.question: What region of the nervous system is predominantly affected in gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: Gluten ataxia predominantly affects the cerebellum, with loss of Purkinje cells, which explains the symptoms of incoordination and dysmetria.

questions.question: Which brain MRI finding is characteristic of gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: Diffuse cerebellar atrophy, especially of the vermis, is the most characteristic neuroimaging finding in advanced gluten ataxia.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with celiac disease can present gluten ataxia?
questions.explanation: It is estimated that between 5% and 10% of patients with celiac disease develop gluten ataxia, being one of the most frequent neurological manifestations.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Celiac disease is an autoimmune enteropathy triggered by the ingestion of gluten (gliadin) in genetically susceptible individuals, causing damage to the intestinal mucosa.

questions.question: What is the characteristic histological finding in the duodenal biopsy of a patient with active celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Intestinal villous atrophy (classified as Marsh 3) is the classic histological finding, along with crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytic infiltrate.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: A lifelong gluten-free diet is the only effective treatment, achieving clinical and histological remission in the majority of patients with celiac disease.

questions.question: Which of the following antibodies is the most sensitive and specific for the initial diagnosis of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: The anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibody is the first-line serological test due to its high sensitivity and specificity (>95%) in patients with gluten ingestion.

questions.question: Which of the following clinical manifestations is typical of the classic malabsorption syndrome in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Steatorrhea (fatty, bulky, malodorous stools) along with weight loss are cardinal manifestations of the classic malabsorption syndrome in celiac disease.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of lack of response to a gluten-free diet in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: The most common cause of non-response is the inadvertent or voluntary ingestion of gluten, either hidden in processed foods or due to cross contamination.

questions.question: What is the definitive treatment for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: A lifelong gluten-free diet is the only effective treatment for celiac disease, as it eliminates the immune trigger and allows the intestinal mucosa to recover.

questions.question: What happens if a celiac patient inadvertently consumes gluten?
questions.explanation: The inadvertent ingestion of gluten in a celiac patient triggers the immune response again, causing inflammation and damage to the intestinal mucosa, which can perpetuate symptoms and complications.

questions.question: Which of the following foods should a patient with celiac disease avoid?
questions.explanation: Whole wheat bread contains gluten, a protein that triggers the autoimmune response in celiac disease, so it should be excluded from the diet for life.

questions.question: What is a possible consequence of not adhering to the gluten-free diet?
questions.explanation: Continued exposure to gluten in celiac disease increases the risk of long-term complications, such as intestinal lymphoma, osteoporosis, and other autoimmune diseases.

questions.question: What antibody is used to monitor adherence to the gluten-free diet?
questions.explanation: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibody is the main serological marker to evaluate the response to the gluten-free diet; Its decrease indicates good adhesion and mucosal recovery.

questions.question: When is the reintroduction of gluten in a celiac patient considered safe?
options: Never, the diet is for life
questions.explanation: The gluten-free diet must be maintained for life in celiac disease, since the reintroduction of gluten, even after clinical remission, triggers intestinal damage and complications again.

questions.question: What is the type of lymphoma most associated with long-standing celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) is the most common lymphoid complication in patients with long-standing celiac disease, especially in those with poor adherence to a gluten-free diet.

questions.question: What factor is associated with a higher risk of intestinal lymphoma in celiac patients?
options: Age of diagnosis less than 2 years
questions.explanation: Refractory celiac disease type II, characterized by aberrant intraepithelial lymphocytes, confers a significantly elevated risk of developing intestinal T-cell lymphoma, unlike other conditions.

questions.question: What is the most common location of intestinal lymphoma in celiac patients?
questions.explanation: Lymphoma associated with celiac disease usually originates in the jejunum and proximal ileum, areas of greatest immunological activity and chronic mucosal damage, with the jejunum being the most classically described site.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic of T-cell lymphoma associated with enteropathy?
questions.explanation: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma presents aberrant CD3+ CD8- (double negative) T lymphocytes with an intraepithelial phenotype, unlike other intestinal lymphomas that usually express CD8 or CD20.

questions.question: What symptom should alert you to possible lymphoma in a celiac patient?
questions.explanation: Involuntary weight loss accompanied by fever or night sweats in a celiac patient, especially if they do not respond to a gluten-free diet, are warning signs to rule out intestinal lymphoma.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma?
questions.explanation: The CHOP chemotherapy regimen (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) is the first-line treatment for enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma, although the prognosis remains guarded.

questions.question: What is the most common location of villous atrophy in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Celiac disease predominantly affects the duodenum, where biopsy shows characteristic villous atrophy.

questions.question: What histological finding is key for the diagnosis of celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Villous atrophy in duodenal biopsy is the fundamental histological finding to confirm celiac disease.

questions.question: What is the main triggering factor for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Gluten, present in wheat, barley and rye, is the immunological trigger of celiac disease.

questions.question: What antibody is used as first-line serological screening in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA is the antibody of choice for initial screening of celiac disease.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: The strict gluten-free diet is the only effective, first-line treatment for celiac disease.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recognized complication of untreated celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma is a serious complication of untreated celiac disease.

questions.question: What is the main cause of refractory iron deficiency anemia in adults?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia refractory to treatment with oral iron is usually due to malabsorption, with celiac disease being a classic and common cause in adults.

questions.question: What test confirms the diagnosis of celiac disease in refractory anemia?
questions.explanation: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies are the first-line serological test for celiac disease, essential for iron deficiency anemia without response to iron.

questions.question: What is the pathophysiological mechanism of anemia in celiac disease?
options: Villous atrophy that reduces iron absorption
Increased blood loss due to ulcers
questions.explanation: Intestinal villous atrophy in celiac disease decreases the surface area for nutrient absorption, especially iron, causing refractory iron deficiency anemia.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for iron deficiency anemia due to celiac disease?
questions.explanation: The gluten-free diet is the fundamental treatment for celiac disease; By restoring the intestinal mucosa, it allows normal absorption of iron and corrects anemia.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding suggests refractory iron deficiency anemia due to malabsorption?
options: Low ferritin with no response to oral iron
questions.explanation: Low ferritin indicates iron deficiency; Lack of response to oral iron points to malabsorption, as in celiac disease, ruling out other causes.

questions.question: What condition is associated with iron deficiency anemia that does not respond to oral iron?
questions.explanation: Celiac disease is a recognized cause of refractory iron deficiency anemia due to iron malabsorption, differentiating it from other anemias that present with normal or elevated ferritin.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of osteoporosis in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: In celiac disease, villous atrophy reduces intestinal calcium absorption, leading to negative calcium balance and bone loss.

questions.question: Which laboratory test is usually altered in osteoporosis due to calcium malabsorption?
questions.explanation: Calcium malabsorption produces mild hypocalcemia, which stimulates the secretion of PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) to mobilize bone calcium.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for osteoporosis in celiac disease?
questions.explanation: The strict gluten-free diet allows the recovery of the intestinal mucosa, correcting calcium malabsorption and improving bone mineral density.

questions.question: What serological marker is associated with active celiac disease?
questions.explanation: IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) antibodies are the most sensitive and specific serological marker for the diagnosis of active celiac disease.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic in duodenal biopsy for celiac disease?
questions.explanation: Intestinal villous atrophy (Marsh 3) is the classic histological finding of celiac disease, along with crypt hyperplasia and lymphocytic infiltrate.

questions.question: What vitamin is deficient due to malabsorption in untreated celiac patients?
questions.explanation: Calcium malabsorption in celiac patients is frequently associated with vitamin D deficiency, which aggravates osteoporosis by reducing intestinal absorption of calcium.
